attribution.text: Route, junction, and history details adapted from the Wikipedia article "Rogue-Umpqua Scenic Byway", by Wikipedia contributors, licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

sources.name: Federal Highway Administration — National Scenic Byways

itinerary.note: Junction toward Crater Lake & Bend OR 230 begins

paragraphs: The road was designated a National Forest Scenic Byway on October 21, 1990, by the National Forest Service. It was later named an Oregon State Scenic Byway on February 19, 1997, and a National Scenic Byway on June 13, 2002.

highlights.d: Junction toward Crater Lake & Bend OR 230 begins

references.text: "Rogue-Umpqua Scenic Byway". U.S. Bureau of Land Management. Retrieved May 3, 2011.
"Rogue-Umpqua Scenic Byway". Travel Oregon. Retrieved May 3, 2011.
"Rogue-Umpqua Scenic Byway: Official Designations". America's Byways. Federal Highway Administration. Retrieved May 4, 2011.
"Road Assets and Mileage". Oregon Department of Transportation. Retrieved January 14, 2021.

pois.name: Joseph H Stewart County Park Campground

gallery.desc: A new culvert is put in place along Oregon 38 in Douglas County.
gallery.credit: Oregon Department of Transportation · CC BY 2.0

gallery.desc: A new culvert is put in place along Oregon 38 in Douglas County.
gallery.credit: Oregon Department of Transportation · CC BY 2.0

gallery.desc: A line up of cars awaits Jaguar lovers at the Jag Tour. Status: Public domain. Photo by Christine Lilienthal
Jaguars of a variety of colors and styles line up for perusal. Status: Public domain. Photo by Christine Lilienthal

gallery.cap: Autumn at Susan Creek Recreation Area
gallery.desc: Located in the heart of the Western Oregon Cascade Mountains, this recreation site is one of the most accessible sites managed by the Bureau of Land Management for people with disabilities . Travel is enjoyable in any season and frequent mist creates captivating views. Location: Oregon Status: Public domain. Courtesy of Ron Murphy of the BLM

gallery.cap: Autumn Reflections on the North Umpqua River
gallery.desc: Vibrant autumn color reflects on the pools of the North Umpqua River where beauty is abundant year-round. Pools intermingle with rapids and riffles creating opportunities to watch migrating fish seasonally leap through the fast water stretches and glide effortlessly in the pools. Location: Oregon Status: Public domain. Courtesy Ron Murphy of the BLM
A black and white photo portrays the quiet beauty of Diamond Lake. Status: Public domain. Photo by Christine Lilienthal

gallery.cap: Byway Signage Off I 5 by Gold Hill
gallery.desc: An Oregon state byway sign and an America's Byways sign mark the southwestern terminus to the Rogue-Umpqua Scenic Byway. Location: Gold Hill, Oregon (42.426° N 123.040° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane
gallery.credit: A. E. Crane, U.S. Department of Transportation · Public domain

gallery.cap: Driving Past Table Rocks in Southern Oregon
gallery.desc: The Rogue-Umpqua Scenic Byway (SR 234) runs straight and smooth through the farm and ranchland past both Table Rocks. Location: Sams Valley, Oregon (42.480° N 122.960° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane
gallery.credit: A. E. Crane, U.S. Department of Transportation · Public domain

gallery.cap: Entrance to Joseph Stewart State Park
gallery.desc: A brown and gold sign highlighted by the blue logo of the Oregon State Parks Program stands out against the tall conifers at the road leading to Joseph Stewart State Park. Location: Steward State Park, Oregon (42.674° N 122.626° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane
gallery.credit: A. E. Crane, U.S. Department of Transportation · Public domain

gallery.desc: Signs help guide travelers to their destination in the Casey Recreation Area at Rogue Elk County Park. Location: Rogue Elk County Park, Oregon (42.652° N 122.720° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane
gallery.credit: A. E. Crane, U.S. Department of Transportation · Public domain

gallery.desc: Known as the "Emerald Jewel" of Oregon, kayakers can experience whitewater thrills in the 33.8 mile North Umpqua Wild Scenic River corridor. With an average drop of 26 feet per river mile, the Class V river offers year-round kayaking excitement. Location: Oregon Status: Public domain. Courtesy of USDI-BLM

gallery.cap: Looking towards Mt. Thielsen from the Byway
gallery.desc: Stop at the Mt. Thielsen Viewpoint along Highway 230 and see this ancient volcano towering at 9,182-feet high. Status: Public domain. Photo courtesy of Gregg Morgan of BLM

gallery.cap: McGregor Park Fishing Area at Lost Creek Reservoir
gallery.desc: One of the features of McGregor Park near Lost Creek Reservoir is a simple fishing area near SR 62 and Takelma Drive, which has paths to the river, a latrine and paved parking. Location: Lost Creek Reservoir Area, Oregon (42.659° N 122.694° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane
gallery.credit: A. E. Crane, U.S. Department of Transportation · Public domain

gallery.cap: Natural Bridge and the Hidden River
gallery.desc: The "Hidden River" emerges from the lava flow that forms the Natural Bridge. Location: Rogue River National Forest, Oregon (42.889° N 122.466° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane
gallery.credit: A. E. Crane, U.S. Department of Transportation · Public domain

gallery.desc: Rafters enjoy floating along a calm stretch of the Rogue River near Rogue Elk County Park. Location: Rogue Elk County Park, Oregon (42.652° N 122.749° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane
gallery.credit: A. E. Crane, U.S. Department of Transportation · Public domain

gallery.desc: The Rogue-Umpqua Scenic Byway parallels 73 miles of two national wild and scenic rivers, the Upper Rogue and North Umpqua. Outstanding scenery, high quality access, and diverse recreational opportunities occur in this ideal setting. This particular stretch of road passes through the Umpqua National Forest. Location: Oregon Status: Public domain. Courtesy of USFS

gallery.cap: Store and Cafe at Stewart State Park Marina
gallery.desc: A rustic store and cafe at the marina provides meals, supplies, equipment and other necessities to the visitors to Joseph Stewart State Park. Location: Joseph Stewart State Park, Oregon (42.678° N 122.635° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane
gallery.credit: A. E. Crane, U.S. Department of Transportation · Public domain
